item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bit late here but I genuinely do like the little twist of expectations at the end. Building up Tactical Theology as this group trying to game religious rules with no respect for the faith and then revealing that the holy men aren't much different.

I like that. I like when short articles do the twist thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One the one hand, the whole "let's string together a series of theological rules and principles to achieve an engineering solution" is very much up my alley.

On the other hand:

  • The initial postulate is that there's an anomalous body in the Hoover Dam that happens to be anomalously well-preserved. And is also an Elvis impersonator. Although I understand why the body has to be incorruptible for this concept to work, to me it's a distracting omission how the anomaly is just dropped in there and never explained or explored. This could have been easily dealt with by, for example, saying that Mr. Simmons was accidentally killed during a particular anomalous occurrence, which explains the residual anomalous effect of the body being incorruptible.
  • The stinger about how the cardinal wants to knock boots with a succubus is, well, kind of a groaner. It makes the character of the cardinal into a caricature that a 14-year-old would make up.
why just, why

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Biblical canon is that every man who has ever lived indulges in sin, so I’d say yes. Otherwise there would be no canonized saints.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For science, of course.

item.title: Re: Thaumielvis
item.content:encoded:
OK yes, thanks for the help. It just wasn't a topic that would target my sense of humor, it seems. I had understood what was going on, but just didn't think of it as comedic, just as an murky ethics business arrangement.

I suppose I've read so much SCP that the clinical tone has bled through into my sense of (lack of?) humor. Huh. How about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First the Foundation publishes hentai, now it's playing pimp for cardinals. It's been a weird year. +1

item.title: Re: Thaumielvis
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

The SCP is a body lodged in the Hoover Dam. The Foundation needs holy water to fight demons in Las Vegas. Since water that touches a saint becomes holy water, the Foundation asks a cardinal to quietly canonize the body and make it a saint. Then they have an unlimited supply of holy water, as all the water that touches the body comes from the Hoover Dam.

In return, the cardinal asks for an intimate encounter with a succubus. Succubi are described as demons that enter intimate encounters quite frequently. Since the cardinal has taken the vow of chastity upon becoming a priest, he cannot enter an intimate encounter if he follows his vow. If he doesn't follow the vow and word leaks out, he'll be sacked from his post as a cardinal. So in return for canonizing the SCP, the Foundation will help distract the cardinal's guards, suppress information, and help the cardinal meet with a succubus. If you Google the term "succubus", you will also understand what the succubus does very quickly.

The joke is all of this hidden subtext and what Ruini is trying to say without saying it. Also, before Ruini mentions succubi, people may assume that he wants to fight a demon or something like that. When he does mention succubi, the surprise is funny.

(I'm extremely squeamish about mentioning any specific details of what the succubus and the cardinal are going to do.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
- Y. Leiner
Abrahamic Subdepartment Lead

HMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMMM

Yep that's my author avatar now.

That being said, this was incredibly enjoyable. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I swear this entire SCP was an excuse to use the pun "Thaumielvis".

item.title: Re: Thaumielvis
item.content:encoded:
I'm so very sorry but I am going to have to be "that guy" who politely requests that a joke be explained to him, which ruins the joke.

Because I didn't get the joke.
Wasn't even aware that there was a joke.

I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The phrase “tactical theology” is so cool I got lightheaded. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this building to a canon? I would very much enjoy a high roller, city of sin, man that broke the bank, "what happens in vegas stays in vegas" style canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A good work, I enjoyed the reading a lot.

+1

Saint Elvis, patron saint of the rock and roll

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I imagine reading the vegas article will help drastically

Well, yeah. It mentions that the events that occurred in the Vegas article are why they need the water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: finally a perfect scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you're supposed to infer that "tartarean entities" can only be combatted with holy water, and then the big notice mentions the theory from the department of tactical theology that the body not decomposing means its "incorruptible" (presumably by sin), or at least that it can be argued well enough that a big official catholic CEO type dude (i do not understand the hierarchy of catholicism) would be able to "canonize" the body. Then I'm pretty sure canonization means making someone a saint or priest? And then once the body is a priest, any water it touches become holy, submerge it into a large body of water, infinite ammo for combating tartarean entities, QED.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm quite torn about this one. To me, there is no obvious connection between a dead body which doesn't decompose and a need for holy water (I get that his body is submerged in water, but there still doesn't seem to be a connection). I imagine reading the vegas article will help drastically, but I'm not sure. That is why I had it downvoted, but it's entirely possible that I missed something incredibly obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indulgences, baby.

item.title: Thaumielvis
item.content:encoded:
Oh god, the Succubi joke got me. Anyways, I really enjoyed the world building in this, like how Vegas going to hell was a rumor and all the religion-y stuff was pretty cool. Who would've thought basically blessing and pushing a dead Elvis impersonator into the Hoover Dam would un-hell Las Vegas?

oh and also Thaumielvis. Thaumielvis is great. +1

edit: fuck you rounder i fixed my spelling mistake <3

edit2: fuck you too ARD i fixed my other spelling mistake <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can a priest who indulges in sin still canonize a cadaver?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff. Great side piece to 4661 and adds some much needed world building to the whole endeavor. Punchline is exactly the kind of self-indulgent dry humor I appreciate in articles like these.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I really dig this.

I've said it before, but the fusion of religious and 'scientific' themes that are present in a good number of skips are something I've always really enjoyed, and this is no exception. The idea of gaming the canonization system in order to get an infinite supply of holy water is really creative, and the world-building this does in relation to 4661 is also a really nice touch.

The interview, while short, is filled with character - you really get the sense that there's a history between them here - and the reveal at the end of what exactly Ruini is after here is a great comic beat.

+1

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to Tanhony or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good skip, but shouldn't 4661 be crosslinked here?

Following an increase in Tartarean entities manifesting in the Las Vegas area

EDIT: Found the link, silly me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
image is here and cc by sa 4.0

This article takes place in the same canon as SCP-4661, which still doesn't have a name. Yay world building.
Yay.
Thank you to Kirby, Vezaz, and Rounder for reading.
